title: Pyrography ball tip shading
subtitle: Using the ball stylus tip for pointillist texture and tonal shading
excerpt: A technique entry covering how to use the pyrography ball stylus tip for dotwork, fur and feather texture, and smooth tonal gradients. The ball tip is a complement to the writing and shading tips.
sourceNotes: General pyrography method synthesised from standard craft practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Decorating wood with a heated metal tip. The ball stylus is one of the standard tips in the pyrography toolkit.
Building areas of light, mid, and dark tone. The ball tip achieves this through dot density: closely spaced dots produce a darker tone than widely spaced ones.

content.text: The
pyrography

ball stylus tip is a spherical steel ball welded to the wire or mounted on the solid-tip unit. It produces a round dot rather than a line. The dot size and darkness depends on the temperature and contact time.
Tonal shading

with the ball tip is achieved by varying the dot density: closely packed dots at the same temperature produce a darker area than widely spaced dots. The ball tip is the correct tool for animal fur and feather texture, for stippled backgrounds, and for smooth gradients where the linear shading tip leaves a striped texture.

Ventilation: open a window minimum, run an extractor fan if possible. Wood smoke is a respiratory irritant; some woods release toxic fumes (yew, oleander, treated lumber). Stick to the allowed-woods list.
Heat: the burner unit stays hot for fifteen minutes after switch-off. Park it on a dedicated metal stand, not a bench surface.
Eye protection: safety glasses when grinding or shaping tips.
Fume sensitivity: stop and ventilate the room if your throat feels scratchy.
Temperature and contact time

The ball tip holds more heat per contact than the writing tip because of its mass. Start at a lower temperature than you would use for outline work. Contact time is the other variable: the longer the ball rests on the surface, the darker the dot. For a consistent dot size, keep the contact time constant and adjust the temperature only.
Dotwork (pointillism)
Set the burner to a low-medium temperature. Test on scrap: a single contact of about half a second should produce a pale brown dot 1.5 to 2 mm in diameter.
Work dots in rows for a background fill. Space the dots evenly, with gaps equal to the dot diameter for a medium tone. Close the gaps for a darker tone; widen them for a lighter tone.
For a gradient from dark to light, start with closely spaced dots at the dark end and increase the spacing gradually as you work toward the light end.
Fur and feather texture
Place the ball tip on the surface and drag it in a very short stroke (2 to 3 mm) in the direction the fur or feathers lie. This produces a short comma-shaped mark rather than a round dot.
Work in rows following the fur or feather direction. Overlap rows slightly. The comma shapes read as individual fur strands at viewing distance.
Build darker areas by increasing the temperature for one or two additional passes in the shadow areas.
Combining with other tips

The ball tip works best after the writing tip has established the outline and main tonal areas. Use the writing tip for outlines and the darkest details; then the shading tip for large smooth tone areas; then the ball tip for texture and fine tonal gradients. The three tips complement each other.

items.symptom: Dots are different sizes within the same area
items.cause: Variable contact time; the hand is not consistent
items.fix: Count a rhythm for each dot: one-two for a light dot, one-two-three for a medium dot. Consistent counting produces consistent dots.

items.symptom: Fur strokes produce blobs instead of commas
items.cause: Temperature too high; the ball is burning too dark before being dragged
items.fix: Reduce the temperature. The drag stroke must happen while the surface is still dark from the initial contact, not after the initial contact has already burned a full dark dot.

items.symptom: Ball tip sticks to the wood surface
items.cause: Temperature too high; the tip is partially fusing with the charred wood fibres
items.fix: Reduce the temperature significantly. A sticking tip means the surface is over-burned. Allow the area to cool before continuing.
